Hi Martin,

Thanks - let me look into where that dictionary merge happens as it wasn't immediately obvious to me.  Did you have a pointer to which part you meant by any chance please?

I don't think that we need different behaviour (in this case) for Y1.1 vs. Y1 (i.e., this fix is not intended to implement the 7950 change to not need to import groupings from a sibling) but rather specifically to deal with the example case that I provided (main module defines a grouping which is then used in in a submodule). AIUI, a Y1 implementation should allow this grouping to be used for two reasons:

1) The text in 6020 Section 5.1 doesn't define any requirement for a submodule to specify any statement to use its parent's nodes, which are in its own namespace, so I assume that they must be accessible:

```
   The import and include statements are used to make definitions
   available to other modules and submodules:

   o  For a module or submodule to reference definitions in an external
      module, the external module MUST be imported.

   o  For a module to reference definitions in one of its submodules,
      the module MUST include the submodule.

   o  For a submodule to reference definitions in a second submodule of
      the same module, the first submodule MUST include the second
      submodule.

   There MUST NOT be any circular chains of imports or includes.  For
   example, if submodule "a" includes submodule "b", "b" cannot include
   "a".
```

2) There is no language mechanism that one can use to fix this AIUI. The options would be that in the submodule we specify `include main-module`, but this will introduce a circular dependency (sub->main->sub); or that we specify an `import main-module`, but again, this is a circular dependency.

So at least for the case where a submodule can reference something in its parent, then I don't think there's any need to specifically handle Y1 vs Y1.1. Do you agree?

Thanks,

Bastien:

I am very impressed with the way you have come up to speed on the codebase, not only getting it to do what you want, but also providing a well-coded and useful test.  You have apparently developed an excellent understanding of how the system fits together in the face of very sparse documentation, and I would love for you to become a regular pdfrw contributor.

But...

I am not convinced that six is right for pdfrw.

I suppose my thoughts echo those of [Armin Ronacher](http://lucumr.pocoo.org/2013/5/21/porting-to-python-3-redux/) where he advises to "skip six."

Most of my reluctance to embrace six could be fairly characterized as aesthetic.  For example, I have a visceral negative reaction to the thought of increasing the size of the codebase by over 25%, but I could probably overcome that if someone convinced me that the benefits were worthwhile.

As you point out, there are benefits to using six that can be substantial for some codebases and coding styles.  It is a _de facto_ community standard, well-understood by people who maintain libraries that support 2 and 3, and the new hybrid Python variant it creates allows a far more flexible coding style than the one I have chosen for the bulk of pdfrw.

But while six abstracts away some differences, it does not abstract away all of them.  For example, you propose adding these lines to `PdfReader()`:

            if six.PY3 and isinstance(fdata, six.binary_type):
                fdata = fdata.decode('Latin-1')

This uses tools in six that help to _manage_ the differences between two and three, but it does not actually _hide_ those differences.  In fact,  these lines expose _a lot_ of knowledge about both the differences between Python 2 and 3 and knowledge about six itself (e.g., what is `six.binary_type`?) to a large function in a large file.

The current version of `PdfReader()` knows that there are required differences between data handling for loading files under 2 vs 3, but it doesn't know or care about the details of those differences, and I think that's a good design -- even if I were convinced that the technology in six was a good fit for pdfrw, I would probably still want to take those two lines and place them in a separate function in a separate file where I abstract away those differences so that the `PdfReader()` class doesn't know the details.

If I stick with that decision to abstract away the 2 vs. 3 file handling differences in a separate module, then it seems to me that the value proposition of six is greatly reduced, and on the cost side, if we include six in the package as you have proposed, then we risk running into issues [like this](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/27045039/python-use-different-versions-of-six-module) and if we add a dependency for six as an external module, well, that would be the first external dependency, and that's a pretty high hurdle to clear.

So it seems unlikely you could convince me that six is the right answer for pdfrw as of now.

But did I mention I love your test? :-)  (as well as some of the other changes such as updating for 3.5)

Seriously, I think your test along with the code snippet in issue #38 is what it takes to put that issue to bed, and I really appreciate it.  If you update the PR to insure your test works with that code, that would be awesome, but if you're too busy, I'll understand -- I'm swamped at the moment as well, but I can probably get around to fixing this sometime next week.

Thanks,
Pat

Hi,

I noticed that dlib (v19.8) doesn't compile under c++17 when I include dlib/gui_widgets.h  (I'm using visual studio 2017). After some further investigation I found that there are still a few references to shared_ptr_thread_safe in gui_core_kernel_1:

```
dlib::shared_ptr_thread_safe<gui_core_kernel_1_globals::event_handler_thread> globals; c:\dlib\gui_core\gui_core_kernel_1.h Line 172

const shared_ptr_thread_safe<event_handler_thread>& global_data()	c:\dlib\gui_core\gui_core_kernel_1.cpp Line 311

static shared_ptr_thread_safe<event_handler_thread> p; c:\dlib\gui_core\gui_core_kernel_1.cpp Line 314

```

To get around it, I followed the same fix as in PR #590. I.e. I replaced all occurrences of dlib::shared_ptr_thread_safe with std::shared_ptr and I also removed the include of shared_ptr_thread_safe.h in gui_core_kernel_1. After that, dlib compiles fine under c++17 and it seems to run fine as well. 

However, I then noticed in the comment for global_data() (gui_core_kernel_1.cpp) that there could potentially be problems using std::shared_ptr here instead of dlib::shared_ptr_thread_safe. 
I reckon this is the reason gui_core_kernel_1.h and gui_core_kernel_1.cpp were not updated and replaced with std::shared_ptr in this change?

I'll make a PR for the nodal data in the first place. I attach [here](https://github.com/nschloe/meshio/files/1639089/test-med-data.zip) a test MED file generated by a Code_Aster calculation.
```
points, cells, point_data, cell_data, field_data = meshio.read("test.rmed", file_format="med")
```
should give you a `point_data` containing three fields: the displacement, and stress/strain averaged at nodes (`SIGM_NOEU` and `EPSI_NOEU`) computed by Code_Aster.

Attention, the ordering for these symmetric 2nd order tensors is the following in MED
```
XX YY ZZ XY XZ YZ
```
However for VTU-like formats (to be read in ParaView), we should have
```
XX YY ZZ XY YZ XZ
```
Additional care is to be taken by the user and by the script `meshio-convert`.

No, it actually does not get rid of ``_ForwardRef``. After looking more closely into ``_ForwardRef`` I had learned that it provides fields to store the referenced type, i.e. ``__forward_arg__``, ``__forward_value__`` and ``__forward_evaluated__``. On resolving, ``__forward_value__`` shall be filled with the actal type, ``__forward_arg__`` provides the string representation thereof. Using these fields seems to be the official way and avoids sort of hassle with replacing a type within a potentially complex structure of PEP484 types. The downside is, it allows for cycles, but that's not my invention. ``_issubclass`` should now be cycle proof regarding this. And I think without much additional overhead.

I believe setting `self.tunnel.skip_tunnel_checkup = False` before you start your tunnel will run a check that should fail fast and allow you to retry.

But I've found it's very robust to attempt a connection over the tunnel than to try and check for specific errors. I'd call `self.socket.settimeout()` before you connect your socket to force quick failure, and that has the advantage that it only succeeds when everything is in place.

Also make sure you're closing those failed connections!

Also; when using the Get/Set Attribute Single EtherNet/IP CIP request -- you must use raw SINT data of the size of the target attribute.  No CIP data type can be used; they are not supported by Get/Set Attribute Single/All.

So, I would recommend trying the above call with "@0x09/1/3=(SINT)1" instead, and see what happens.  If you add a  `-vvv` option, we can see what the CIP error code is.

The workaround is to point SourceTree Custom Action to a Bash script that sets the locale and then invokes `git-up`.

[The referenced 'Click' library page](http://click.pocoo.org/python3/) describes why this is needed when using Python 3.

```bash
#!/bin/bash

set -eu

REPO_PATH=$1

if [[ ! -d "${REPO_PATH}" ]]; then
	exit -1
fi

( cd "${REPO_PATH}" && LC_ALL=en_US.utf-8 LANG=en_US.utf-8 /usr/local/bin/git-up )
```

There are 2 configs in `1.4.1`: max_poll_interval_ms and session_timeout_ms. The second one is the time Broker will tolerate no heartbeats and the first one is the time it will tolerate to rejoin the group. If your processing time exceeds the first param Broker will kick you out of the group and subsequent commit will have the error above. Operate `max_poll_interval_ms` to have at least enough time for processing.

I mostly use pygount in combination with Jenkins, where the sum of lines is computed automatically.

With the option `--format=cloc-xml` the XML contains the total count, for example:
```xml
<total blank="221" code="1008" comment="185" />
```

If you just need to know the sum, you can pipe the result of pygount into `awk`, for example:
```sh
pygount some_project | awk '{s+=$1} END {print s}'
```

By default, pygount uses the same format as `sloccount --details --wide`, which does not include a total sum either.

Do these options solve your issue? It would certainly possible to add a command line option like `--total` to only show the sum total of lines or `--summary` to show a break down similar to sloccount's default output but I think these days CI systems like Jenkins are the way to go which already offer an extensive visual break down including historic data.

Guess it really depends on what you're trying to do and how married you are to getting your data out in csv format...

```python
import nflgame
games = nflgame.games(year=2016, week=1, kind='PRE')

for game in games:
    print game
    game.players.passing().csv( "game_data.csv")  # I assume you're doing something like this...

    # If you're married to CSV format and you want all games in separate files you could do something like this:
    game.players.csv('{}@{}game_data.csv'.format(game.away, game.home))
    # will give you a .csv file for every game played in the week

    # You could just as easily be accessing all of the data directly...
    for player in game.players.passing():
        print player, player.formatted_stats()
    # this will print out each passers name with all categories they registered a statistic in
    # ie. T.Savage passing_att: 24, passing_twoptm: 0, passing_twopta: 0, passing_yds: 168, passing_cmp: 14, passing_ints: 0, passing_tds: 2

    # Or just the data you were interested in...
    for player in game.players.passing():  
        print '{} threw {} completions in {} attempts ({:0.2f}%)'.format(
            player, player.passing_cmp, player.passing_att,
            player.passing_cmp/float(player.passing_att)*100)
    # will print out a formatted string for each passer's completion percentage
    # ie. T.Savage threw 14 completions in 24 attempts (58.33%)

    # You probably know this, but no need to limit yourself to just passers...
    for player in game.players:  
        print player, player.stats
    # this will print each player (regardless of whether they were a passer or not) and stats recorded
    # ie. D.Pettinato OrderedDict([(u'defense_ffum', 0), (u'defense_tkl', 1), (u'defense_int', 0), (u'defense_ast', 0), (u'defense_sk', 0)])
```

If you are truly after the csv formatted data, there are a couple of different things you could do.
```python
# gives you a 3461 line csv file with all players that recorded a stat in week 1 of this preseason
import nflgame
games = nflgame.games(year=2016, week=1, kind='PRE')
players = nflgame.combine_max_stats(games)
players.csv('all_week1_data.csv')
```

```python
# gives you a 2307 line csv file with all players and their aggregated stats over weeks 4, 5, and 6 in 2015
import nflgame
games = nflgame.games(year=2015, week=range(4, 7), kind='PRE')
players = nflgame.combine_max_stats(games)
players.csv('all_weeks456_data.csv')
```

```python
# why stop at a single week or even a range of weeks.  How about the entire season?
import nflgame
games = nflgame.games(year=2015, kind='REG')
players = nflgame.combine_max_stats(games)
players.csv('entire_2015_season.csv')
```

This is supported in Revision 1.6.1 by specifying tags like:

```xml
      <global name="[default]" type="integer" modifiers="allocatable" dimension=":" />
      <assignment name="[default]" constant="0" allocate="size(N)" />
```

The `[default`] name refers to the variable that will store the output of the function; it can have `<assignment>` tags reference it to allocate or change its value etc. just like any variable can, the treatment is equivalent.

Python 2.7:
```
>>> '\r' == b'\r'
True
```
Python 3:
```
>>> '\r' == b'\r'
False
```

@likohank most of your observation about how 32-bit python behaves unders 64-bit windows is irrelevant. Microsoft Windows internally does a few things when runnng 32-bit applications on the 64-bit OS. One thing is that registry access is mapped to a different sub-tree ("WOW64Node" ), and another is that attempts to access %SYSTEM32 is remapped from \windows\system32 to \windows\syswow64. This is a general behavior of running 32-bit applications on 64-bit windows.
I assume this thread is about running 64-bit python - you cannot load 32-bit freetype dll with 64-bit python app, nor 64-bit freetype dll with 32-bit python app.

It isn't possible to flush a partial xlsx file since it isn't a sequential file like a text file or CSV file. 

Instead it is a number of XML files in a Zip container. So it can't be saved until all the constituent parts are assembled and it is zipped up.

The C implementation works the same way btw, and has the same behavior there. It turns out that it's handiest to remember the choice selection separately.

Imagine if someone calls `set_value("y")` on a bunch of symbols in the same choice for example. In that case, what you probably want is to have the last symbol set to `y` be the user selection. At the same time, you don't want to have to update the user values of all the other choice symbols, as it gets messy in practice. It's also nice to have `Symbol.user_value` always reflect the assigned value.

There's a bunch of trickiness related to choices that isn't obvious until you try to implement them. Kconfiglib 1 handled them in a messier way, so I know the pain.

Very interesting. Thanks for the detailed benchmarking. I have a point of clarification and then an hypothesis. :-)

First, it sounds like you're under the impression that a lookup happens at parse time every time we delegate to another rule. This is actually not so; Parsimonious builds an object graph of rules when a grammar is compiled (which happens at construction), and simple rules like `foo = "bar"` get hardwired into rules that use them, so these 2 would be object-wise equivalent:

```
snam = foo "hoo" "hoo"
foo = "bar"
```
```
snam = "bar" "hoo" "hoo"
```

However, more complex rules, ones involving a sequence like your `something = thing Spacing` rules, while they still get wired into their users, have an effect on the shape of the graph: they add a Sequence expression that stays around, something like this:

```
Mapping  = SEQUENCE_OF( SEQUENCE_OF(DQString Spacing) SEQUENCE_OF(":" Spacing) Value )
```

It's perfectly possible that the performance consequences of going in and out of those additional Sequences are worse. It would be interesting to compare profiles of your 2 grammars and see where the hotspots lie. I'm going to leave this open as a tantalizing ticket for interested spelunkers.

I think the cut operator and naughty-or are similar. There's also a paper on a throw operator: http://www.inf.puc-rio.br/~roberto/docs/sblp2013-1.pdf.

I built a PEG parser generator in PHP, and I added the cut operator for improved error reporting. The idea is: once you pass the cut operator, you switch from warning mode to error mode. If the parser fails, it will report the expectations that failed (e.g. expected ';') with the highest position (e.g. line 10, character 3). In practice, this can mean that you have a lot of options to choose from (e.g. expected '(' or '[' or ';' or...).

So here's the trick: if you have expectations that failed in error mode, these take precedence over expectations that failed in warning mode.

So naughty-or is good for error recovery. The cut operator is good for error reporting.

See also: https://github.com/scato/phpeg/blob/master/doc/error-reporting.md

I think that your syntax is incorrect.  Notice that in the pdf you produced that the image attributes are not processed.  Pandoc normally does its own image attribute processing.  The fact it didn't in this case tells us that pandoc is not happy with what you wrote.

Here is an example of correct reference link syntax from the pandoc-fignos test suite:

~~~
![Number three. Reference to +@fig:1.][ref-link]

[ref-link]: img/fig-3.png {#fig:3 width=1in}
~~~

Notice that the attributes belong with the link.  I would agree that this is counter-intuitive, but it does appear to be consistent with the pandoc docs (see the section on [link_attributes](https://pandoc.org/MANUAL.html#extension-link_attributes)).

the following solution (commit b4b97d0dd43192848407bce4aa3eb8f6779b456f) works if the user is using pip. it adds code to setup.py to try to manually install eigency in setup.py using pip, reporting an error if it fails: 

```python
# the setup file relies on eigency to import its include paths for the
# extension modules. however eigency isn't known as a dependency until after
# setup is parsed; so we need to check for and install eigency before setup.
import importlib
try:
    importlib.import_module('eigency')
except ImportError:
    try:
        import pip
        pip.main(['install', 'eigency'])
    except ImportError:
        raise ImportError('The eigency library must be installed before FEW. '
                          'Automatic install with pip failed.')
finally:
    globals()['eigency'] = importlib.import_module('eigency')
```

First "IS_PROCESSING_COMPLETE"
I think this is a race condition in the Logic software. Try adding a 100 ms wait after the capture command, before calling IS_PROCESSING_COMPLETE.

Basically, the capture command ACKs right after the capture is complete, but the software is still running some housekeeping to get the capture data to the screen and the UI state updated.
"IS_PROCESSING_COMPLETE" checks the UI state to make sure that the capture tab is active and has data, but this won't happen until a split second after the capture completes.

Alternatively, instead of adding the wait statement, the first NAK exception could just be ignored.

About the second issue "CAPTURE_TO_FILE": does the capture still start in the software? It will NAK if there is a problem with the command, or if a capture error occurred and no data was recorded.

Also, is the <user_path> path mentioned here absolute? It can't start with ~\, our software doesn't support that. I really should fix that.

There's several problems with your code. 

First, don't try to use multiprocessing inside of a class like this. Having class-level flags like `self.can_begain_compare` just isn't going to work in a multithreaded program with multiple threads accessing it at the same time. Instead, have a single function outside of a class that gets run by each thread and make that function take in a fixed set of inputs and return a fixed result. The code that runs in the thread should not access any global/class variables.

Second, you are only calling `executor.submit` once on an empty array, not on an image from the webcam. You aren't ever grabbing new images from the webcam or passing them to the executor from what I can see. And you aren't ever getting the result of the future that you asked it to process.

Third, using a thread pool probably won't work well inside of python anyway because of how it has a global interpreter lock across all threads. You probably won't gain that much real-world performance increase. You'd probably get a better result using a ProcessPool instead. This is just a guess though - I haven't tested it with a thread pool.

But even if you get that working, you have a problem of synchronizing the order of frames that you get back from the threads (assuming that you have multiple threads doing the face recognition step in parallel). The threads are going to finish out of order because different frames will need different amounts of processing. If you show each frame of video as a thread finishes processing it, your final video will jump around and the frames will be out of order. You need some way of keeping the results in order even if they are processed out of order. And just putting all the frames in a queue will cause "frame pacing" freezes in your video where the video speeds up and slows down in a weird way as different frames take more or less time to process by different threads.

In other words, using threads to process video can involve writing some fairly complicated synchronization code or coming up with a more complex design where different threads do different steps of processing (like one thread to do face location, one to do face encoding, etc). It's beyond the scope of something I can answer in a github issue.

I've pushed up a change to make the default expiration time for static
files 1 hour.

I've also had a look at treating hashed named differently, but it's
unfortunately not straightforward. Unlike whitenoise, django-s3-storage has
to decide the cache time when the file is being saved, as opposed to when
it is being retrieved. This means that the internal mapping of named to
cached names doesn't exist, so the trick of asking the storage to
regenerate the hash won't work.

The only thing it has access to is the filename. This means that it would
have to download the file from S3 and regenerate the hash, which is going
to double the time taken to run collectstatic. I'm not sure that this is a
good idea.

Not sure where the bottleneck is occurring, but a wild idea to try is to do a few steps of wavelet downsample (each step halves an axis, so the volume drops by 1/8), send over the reduced data and render that at first, and then over-write. 

Fast DWT tends to be really fast - often faster than FFT. 
```

ary.shape
(332, 500, 500)

print(ary2.shape)

(166, 250, 250)

1 loop, best of 3: 4.8 s per loop

%%timeit

ary2 = pywt.dwtn(ary, 'haar')
print(ary2['aaa'].shape)

(166, 250, 250)
(166, 250, 250)
(166, 250, 250)
1 loop, best of 3: 1.6 s per loop


%%timeit
ary2 = sp.ndimage.zoom(ary, (.25,.25,.25))
print(ary2.shape)

(83, 125, 125)
(83, 125, 125)
(83, 125, 125)
1 loop, best of 3: 2.46 s per loop


%%timeit
​
ary2 = pywt.dwtn(ary, 'haar')
ary3 = pywt.dwtn(ary2['aaa'], 'haar')
print(ary3['aaa'].shape)
(83, 125, 125)
(83, 125, 125)
(83, 125, 125)
1 loop, best of 3: 1.77 s per loop
```

Hello,
Thanks for reporting here.

It is indeed a consequence of the internals of hvad. I am sorry this is not very easy to find in the documentation, but the way hvad handles saves is:
  * Save main model.
  * Copy main model's id into cached translation.
  * Save cached translation.

It has to be done that way for many reasons, such as newly created objects not having an idea until that point. In your case, however, it bites because the translation that is currently loaded will move, as you noticed.

There are two ways to fix this.
* The dirty way would be to reach out for cached translation's id and delete it: get_cached_translation(obj).pk = None`.
* A cleaner way would be not to load the translation at all in the first place: `MyModel.objects.untranslated().first()` should do the trick.

As a rule of thumb, code that is translation-aware and manipulates translations explicitly should load `untranslated()` models to skip hvad special processing of loaded translation.

I am sorry this hit you, and hope the explanation will help you swiftly solve the issue.

@jvrobert File I/O is not something that can be made async, because the OS support is not at all consistent between platforms. Windows (of all things!) has pretty good support for async file I/O, but Linux is kind of a mess, there isn't a good way to do it, unfortunately. The commonly accepted method of doing async I/O in Linux is to use a thread pool.

So I have to modify my advice above to accommodate for this requirement. Instead of using all green threads, you can use an Eventlet [thread pool](http://eventlet.net/doc/threading.html#tpool-simple-thread-pool) to offload the file operations to physical threads, while keeping everything else in the green thread domain.

@djpimp2010 you should be able to send events from a bg thread regarding of the async mode you use. In fact, the async mode is usually determined automatically by the extension based on the packages you have installed in your virtualenv, you do not need to specify it unless you want to override the defaults.

Assuming you are using eventlet or gevent, your background thread should be a greenlet, not a regular thread. If you monkey patched the python std library then the thread functions are automatically routed to the greenlet versions. In the thread, you cannot block for too long, if you have a long process you need to allow other greenlets to run by calling `sleep()`. When running in eventlet or gevent, multitasking is cooperative, so make sure you release the CPU to allow everything to run smoothly.

In general, it seems like you need nested API, like /projects/<id>/issues/
(it is not supported out of the box and needs reimplemention of nested operations, maybe i'll implement such support in DRFM, because nested docs are important in mongo)

You can manipulate individual elements:
to create: `project.update(push__events=new_issue)`
to update: `Project.objects.filter(events__issue=issue_id).update(set__events__S=replacement)`
to delete: `project.update(pull__events={id: issue_id})`

Also, you can try contrib/patching from master branch:
to create: PATCH /projects/<p_id> [{ 'path': "/events", 'op': 'push', 'value': { new issue data }}]
to update: PATCH /projects/<p_id>/issues/<i_id> [{ 'path': "/events/S/subfield", 'op': 'set', 'value': something }]
to delete: PATCH /projects/<p_id> [{ 'path': "/events", 'op': 'pull', 'value': { 'id': issue_id }}]

to retrieve single issue, either python-level filtering required,
or dirty hack with `queryset._loaded_fields = QueryFieldList(['events.$'])`

@plypaul SCHEDULED is set at handover from the scheduler to the executor only. It can only be set by the scheduler. It prevents race conditions. Ideally, it should always be the state of the task when it is sent to the executor. It isn't now due to UP_FOR_RETRY being handled by the TI instead of in the scheduler.

QUEUED indicates that a ti is waiting for a slot in the executor from either a pool or max parallelism. It is a bit ambiguous as it is managed in different locations. Some of the questions @jlowin and I have around #1559 are due to this ambiguity and your broader use of QUEUED.

Under what circumstances would ObserveHandler or EventHandler ever be called outside traitlets.py itself?

There's not a simple flag to turn this on right now, but it should be possible to implement. What's difficult is that the command-line parser doesn't actually know what classes it may configure, so it can't determine whether those are valid or not (it can validate the shortcut aliases and flags, just not Class.trait = value). But the Application does ~know this based on its `classes` attribute, which is a *not-necessarily-authoritative* list of classes it *knows* it will configure. If you are building an application that cannot load any user-defined classes, then it can be treated as an authoritative list and fail on unrecognized traits.

Yes. That is by design. We don't attempt nor care to get to 100% code coverage in both Py2 and Py3. In the past we've "tried" and mostly gotten there by adding pragma: nocover to just Py2/Py3 code-paths giving us a false sense of security.

Unfortunately there are a variety of cases where 100% code coverage simply can't be achieved across both major versions, and that is not a use case we care to support either.

However in the right the copied files are _only_ used to bootstrap the environment. We install a site.py that does some logic to fix sys.path and such then it empties sys.modules and execs the real site.py. The sys.path gets fixed so the stdlib gets used from the real python. This means the only times the copies are used is right before the real site.py gets called while we’re setting up the environment.

Just to answer a few of your questions quickly:
* the placeholders are saved through the standard Django formset/inline admin mechanism. That is `PlaceholderEditorInline` and `PlaceholderInlineFormSet`.
* FYI, in `BaseContentItemFormSet`, the `save_new()` and `save_existing()` are overwritten to deal with placeholders too. Those calls are initiated by the standard `BaseModelFormSet.save()`.
* When you change the layout in the client-side (e.g. using the layout box), the client-side will detect which placeholders will become orphaned, and mark those for deletion. It adds a `placeholder-fs-#-DELETE` field so the object is removed on save.
* The Django formset code in `BaseModelFormSet.save_existing_objects()` loops through `BaseFormSet.deleted_forms`. You may want to use that to your advantage to remove orphaned objects.

When writing this code, I've uses step-through debugging in PyCharm to get this code working, because the inner workings of the formset/inlineadmin code is complex. By walking through the formset code - even through `django.forms.models` - this allowed implementing all the save logic without having to resort to completely rewriting `ModelAdmin.change_view()` like others do, since that is horrible to begin with.

The best solution is to filter specific warnings using the `with warnings.catch_warnings():` context manager, but if you want global silencing, this should do the trick at the top of a notebook:

``` python
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore", message='Possible precision loss', append=True)
```

You can omit `append=True` if this is your only filter, but I note it here so you can run this command multiple times with different `message=` strings to build up a list of specific warnings you wish to silence. 

The second answer [here](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/14463277/how-to-disable-python-warnings) is useful if you want more info.

Really, we don't need to initialize these numpy arrays. We could just set `self._overlaps` and `self._boostedOverlaps` to `None`. We build a new array every timestep, so these arrays are never actually used. If we allocate an array in the `__init__` method, it implies that we're going to reuse it.

This would save us from having `numpy.zeros` calls all over the place. Whether we reuse the arrays or not, there only needs to be one code location that calls `numpy.zeros` (or rather, one for each of the two arrays).

Just based on your description: I would prefer not to have an out-of-band packet length. The protocol already contains sufficient information to determine the packet boundaries (#Items in header plus packet payload length item), and `mem_reader` already operates on a stream of concatenated packets, so I don't want to introduce a second serialisation format for stream data. I realise that it'll take a bit more work to reassemble packets when you can't look arbitrarily far ahead in the stream, but I think it's doable.

I presume the reason of the problem is the new lines in the literals for 'schema:description' and 'schema:articleBody'. It was indeed a long discussion back in the RDFa days whether generated literals should be 'normalized', ie, whether spaces and new lines at the beginning and the end of the literal should be removed but, at the end, it was decided not to do that. The microdata conversion followed the same approach also for compatibility. Formally, there is no such normalization step in the algorithmic definition:

https://www.w3.org/TR/microdata-rdf/#h3_algorithm-terms

The SIL Open Font License isn't considered a [GPL-compatible free software license](https://www.gnu.org/licenses/license-list.html.en#GPLCompatibleLicenses) because it isn't a software license at all. However, it allows bundling the fonts with software, and in fact requires doing so if the fonts are sold. [Here's the FSF's take on it:](https://www.gnu.org/licenses/license-list.html.en#SILOFL)

> The Open Font License (including its original release, version 1.0) is a free copyleft license for fonts. Its only unusual requirement is that when selling the font, you must redistribute it bundled with some software, rather than alone. Since a simple Hello World program will satisfy the requirement, it is harmless. Neither we nor SIL recommend the use of this license for anything other than fonts.








This is supported by [the text of the license itself](http://scripts.sil.org/OFL_web): 

> 1) Neither the Font Software nor any of its individual components,
in Original or Modified Versions, may be sold by itself.

> 2) Original or Modified Versions of the Font Software may be bundled,
redistributed and/or sold with any software, provided that each copy
contains the above copyright notice and this license. 

So there shouldn't be a problem using OFL-licensed fonts in software, including software that's GPL-licensed. But even then, the font itself does not become GPL-licensed:

> 5) The Font Software, modified or unmodified, in part or in whole,
*must be distributed entirely under this license, and must not be
distributed under any other license*. The requirement for fonts to
remain under this license does not apply to any document created
using the Font Software.

(Emphasis added.)

So, the OFL still applies to the font itself. The GPL does not.

Sources: 

[GPL-compatible free software license](https://www.gnu.org/licenses/license-list.html.en#GPLCompatibleLicenses)

[SIL Open Font License version 1.1](http://scripts.sil.org/OFL_web)

Another one which I often stumble on is the inability to pass something like `**kwargs` to `__call__`.
Take as an example `fill_value` in this model. It's not needed to initialize the model. Its natural place is to be passed as an argument to `__call__()` so that once the model is initialized I can evaluate it many times with different values of `fill_value`.

Another one is passing non-numerical parameters to the `__init__` method. This is what I incorrectly called "non-fittable" parameters in my email (as I momentarily forgot about fixed).

This also brings up the related issue of wraping functions with such parameters with `custom_model.
If this was possible any of the interpolating functions in scipy would be easily convertable to this kind of model.

I'd be interested to hear what your thoughts on this are.

So this is an issue, but not really as reported. The problem is that `subscriber.list_subscriptions()` is meant to be for the entire project:

```python
In [1]: import google.auth

In [2]: from google.cloud import pubsub_v1

In [3]: _, project = google.auth.default()

In [4]: publisher = pubsub_v1.PublisherClient()

E1215 14:50:02.434013514   11896 ev_epoll1_linux.cc:1261]    Skipping epoll1 becuase GRPC_LINUX_EPOLL is not defined.
E1215 14:50:02.434020956   11896 ev_epollsig_linux.cc:1761]  Skipping epollsig becuase GRPC_LINUX_EPOLL is not defined.

In [5]: subscriber = pubsub_v1.SubscriberClient()

In [6]: subscriber.list_subscriptions?
Signature: subscriber.list_subscriptions(project, ...)
Docstring:
Lists matching subscriptions.

...

Args:
    project (str): The name of the cloud project that subscriptions belong to.
        Format is ``projects/{project}``.
...

In [7]: project_path = subscriber.project_path(project)

In [8]: LS = list(subscriber.list_subscriptions(project_path))

In [9]: len(LS)
Out[9]: 6
```

The method you want is `publisher.list_topic_subscriptions`:

```python
In [10]: topic_path = publisher.topic_path(project, 'abcdef2')

In [11]: LTS = list(publisher.list_topic_subscriptions(topic_path))

In [12]: len(LTS)
Out[12]: 3

In [13]: publisher.list_topic_subscriptions?
Signature: publisher.list_topic_subscriptions(topic, ...)
Docstring:
Lists the name of the subscriptions for this topic.

...

Args:
    topic (str): The name of the topic that subscriptions are attached to.
        Format is ``projects/{project}/topics/{topic}``.
```

The actual issue is that `subscriber.list_subscriptions` doesn't reject requests with the topic path (likely because the topic path starts with the project path):

```python
In [14]: topic_path.startswith(project_path)
Out[14]: True

In [15]: LS_ALSO = list(subscriber.list_subscriptions(topic_path))

In [16]: LS_ALSO == LS
Out[16]: True
```

I think an issue could be filed with the backend to reject those requests with the `topic_path` where a `project_path` should be.

Depending on the server constraints on your query Cloud Datastore may always choose to not return the full result set. You should always depend on the `RunQueryResponse`s `more_results` field to determine if another lookup is necessary (in particular, you will need to use cursors to fetch the remaining data if `more_results == NOT_FINISHED`). Note that the Cloud Datastore client libraries (gcloud-* and App Engine libraries) handle the batching for you.

Usually this happens if your query is a [merge join](https://cloud.google.com/appengine/articles/indexselection#Improved_Query_Planner) (multiple equality filters and no composite index for the query). In this case, your operations [scale with the size of the smallest set of results that match a single filter](https://cloud.google.com/appengine/articles/indexselection#Performance), which can explain the Cloud Datastore backends decided to return only a small number of results.

I agree a machine-readable format would be preferred. I don't think it's feasible at this point and I'd like to start moving this PR forward instead and work on iterating towards a more sane format.

> Which licenses apply to which files?

We already know which license applies to which files as it's per layer and each layer is known at compile time.

> What to do with merged files?

Docker recommends putting data created by and used by the container that you want to be persistent on a docker volume. If things ended there I would agree that it doesn't warrant a property.

It's very easy to forego this recommendation, and instead bind in a host directory, and put persistent data created by and used by the container there. Take, for example, https://github.com/JensErat/docker-seafile, which recommends starting the container with `/srv/seafile` bind mounted to store its binaries:
```bash
docker run -t -i \
  -p 10001:10001 \
  -p 12001:12001 \
  -p 8000:8000 \
  -p 8080:8080 \
  -p 8082:8082 \
  -v /srv/seafile:/opt/seafile \
  jenserat/seafile -- /bin/bash
```
If you're going to write generic scripts to backup container volume data (as I was :)) "bind" type volumes need to be checked, in addition to the docker volumes you can list via `client.volumes.list()`.

First of all, I'd like to apologize for my misguided discussion about `mTypicalPositiveRatio` in the previous PR you had, #21.  I was very new to chardet then and didn't fully understand how it worked.

How everything works is actually documented fairly well [here](http://chardet.readthedocs.org/en/latest/how-it-works.html).

As you can see in the [original Mozilla paper](http://www-archive.mozilla.org/projects/intl/UniversalCharsetDetection.html), `mTypicalPositiveRatio` is the ratio of "the number of occurrences of the 512 most frequently used characters divided by the number of occurrences of the rest of the characters" for a typical document written in the language in question.  It is a poorly named variable for sure, but has little to do with the confidence (except that we can determine the confidence based on how far a document's ratio is from what we expect).

Anyway, the ratio has to be calculated from a "typical" Turkish document (and currently we don't have a tool to do the calculation for you).

Could you explain how you came up with all of the tables in your PR? What files did you analyze to determine the ratio?

In the future I would like to have tools available to make it much simpler for people to add new encodings, but it is currently pretty difficult.

2015-12-10 13:21 GMT+01:00 Alistair Miles <notifications@github.com>:

> @mrocklin <https://github.com/mrocklin> my main scientific motivation is
> the desire to push the limits of interactive genetic data analysis. The
> genetic data I typically work with is very rich and needs to be explored
> from many different angles to build understanding of data quality issues
> and to discover interesting signals in the data.
>
> Some analyses are compute heavy and will always interrupt the flow,
> however a lot of work also goes into preparation, exploration and
> visualisation of data which is less compute heavy, and where I/O can be as
> costly as compute. In these cases, if lots of steps in the workflow each
> take minutes rather than seconds to compute, it very much interrupts the
> flow and limits the number of avenues that can be explored.
>
> The current scale of the data is ~100 million features by ~1000
> observations which is not actually that big, with compressed arrays and
> chunked algorithms it is perfectly possible to do interactive analysis on a
> commodity laptop or desktop. However, the costs of genome sequencing are
> coming down very quickly, and we expect to be working with datasets 10
> times this size within the next couple of years.
>
> The main limitation in my analysis code right now is the fact that all
> computations are done in series. My desktop is quite modern and has 8 cores
> and it pains me to sit there for minutes waiting for a computation to
> finish and watching only 1 of 8 CPUs being used. I think dask is a great
> way for me to start parallelising a lot of the work I do. I particularly
> like the threaded scheduler, because it doesn't incur any of the overhead
> or complexity of working with multiple processes. I.e., it is very simple
> to set up and use, and fits very nicely with interactive analysis on a
> single machine.
>
> I do have bigger machines available but that won't help if I'm not able to
> effectively utilise multiple cores. Also I'm keen to push what is possible
> on commodity machines because it makes this type of data analysis so much
> more accessible to more people. With a laptop and a 2T hard disk I can sit
> on a train and get all kinds of things done. I also have access to a
> compute cluster with a job scheduler (SGE) so I can do big things in
> parallel if needed, but there is a lot of overhead that comes with coding
> things to work against a job scheduler and also monitoring and debugging
> results, so another reason to push what's possible on a single machine with
> multiple cores.
>
> Sorry that's a bit vague, but it really has been transformative for me and
> my colleagues to be able to work interactively with the data we currently
> have, which is mainly down to efficient data structures (numpy, HDF5 and
> bcolz). If we can start parallelising some work via dask I think that will
> further accelerate current work and put us in a great place for the data
> that's coming next.
>
> —

It sounds like you have some other package which depends on a specific version of python-urllib3 + RHEL's backported security fixes.  Unfortunately, I can't force Curator to use plugins from the official RHEL repository.  

I am obliged to ship updated plugins for whatever Curator depends on in the Elasticsearch repository.  This is for security reasons.  If there are collisions, you'll have to do some manual work to install using other repositories.  

The reason you're getting conflicts is that the Elasticsearch repository has newer versions of the plugins available, and yum/rpm wants to use the newest by default.  Curator should work with the older versions, but I can't put old versions of the plugins in the repository just to satisfy RHEL/CentOS.

My recommendation is to remove the Elasticsearch Curator repository and use the default repository.  If there are dependencies not met, install each manually until Curator works.

This is why I recommend using `pip` instead of packages in the official documentation.  It saves a lot of headache in this vein.

Just to repeat what I said in aio-libs/multidict#182, the sdist appveyor upload definitely helps, though it will still cause breakage for tooling that pins the hashes in requirements files, since once the manylinux wheels are uploaded, pip will prefer the wheels over the sdist, and that new hash won't be present in the requirements file.

Really what's needed here is a PyPI RSS feed for "new file uploaded" (which would allow tools to update the pinned hashes each time), however as far as I know this doesn't exist.

As such, the next best solution is for tools like pyup.io to just add a delay (eg 1 hour) so all wheels have been generated. However in this case it would mean a 12-24 hour delay, given the unpredictability of the OS X backlogs, which are holding up the manylinux jobs. (Shame the OS X parts can't be split out to a completely separate Travis config.)

You're right that this session is a separate concept from the session in the message header. A history session generally corresponds to the lifetime of a kernel process, though in IPython I think it's possible to get a new session without restarting the kernel. The idea is that a session contains the sequence of commands that operated on the same namespace. The references to 'lines' was written when we were only thinking about console interfaces; one notebook cell is one 'line' for this purpose.

'Output' is only the plain text representation of the `execute_result`.

The history messaging was designed by exposing the entire IPython API for history access, and as far as I know, nothing uses most of the possibilities. I believe the only history_request that our own frontends make is a 'tail' request with `output=False`, for the console interfaces to fill their local history buffer. We may well simplify it in the next major revision of the message protocol.

> I still prefer to have the logic and complexity of dealing with partial taken out and reduce the number of options accepted in Model constructor

I agree about minimizing bloat in the main API.

As for the actual functionality itself, consider this example:

```python
class User(Model):
    id = IntType()
    name = StringType()
    city = StringType()
    email = EmailType()
```

Let's say there's an existing row in a database:

id   | name      | city     | email
---- | --------- | -------- | ---------
99   | bintoro   | Helsinki | kalle@...

The client sends an update:

```json
PATCH /users/99 HTTP/1.1
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "city": null
}
```

Currently, updating the record using a Schematics instance created out of the payload would reset not only the city but **every** field. To reset just the city, an application would have to manually inspect the initial data.

Alternatively, loading the old data in an instance first and using `import_data` would reset **nothing**, since the incoming `None` is not considered any different from "not present".

I believe this is a very common use case, and therefore models should be able to deal with missing values natively. Introducing a parallel construct like `PartialModel` would add enormous complexity, as you'd then have to define the interactions between them and "regular" models.

> [...] as long as the non-partial use cases are unaffected, I am OK with the change.

Yes, this is the intent. Whether "no value" should be represented as `None` or `Undefined` is completely up to the application, and can be decided on a case-by-case basis.

Some defaults are probably going to change. For example, have you noticed that running `Model.validate()` will turn all `None`s into the respective fields' defaults? I find this completely bizarre and intend to reverse the behavior (i.e., `validate()` would set `apply_defaults=False`).

> One piece of advice, could we not expose `apply_defaults` and `init_values` as options/kwargs?

That's exactly where I'm going with this. If the existing `partial` parameter could work as a shortcut for the two main cases, then the two overrides (`apply_defaults` and `init_values`) wouldn't have to appear anywhere besides the main `import_loop` function.

The way I see it, the main loops in `transforms.py` should document all available switches, while the methods leading up to them could expose only the most relevant options. We'd have a `**kwargs` slot for the more esoteric arguments.

***

I'm still not sure what to think about having the `partial` parameter control the other two. It's not without its problems. In particular, there would be a disconnect as to the meaning of `partial` between `Model.__init__()` and `Model.validate()`.

A more compatible alternative would be to leave `partial` alone and add the `init_values` parameter to the `Model` constructor. Then, `init_values` could by default dictate the `apply_defaults` setting (unless overridden), since those two definitely go hand in hand.

That way,
  * the `required` check could continue to be performed upon import
  * `partial=True` could remain the default
  * the semantics of `partial` would be preserved exactly as is
  * creating a blank instance would only require switching `init_values` to False.

I set up a parallel branch that implements this and didn't run into any surprises. I think this is a better approach. Adding one more option to `Model.__init__` is not too bad. Those who don't need it can ignore it.

Hey @MikiSoft: the limits themselves could be put directly into the querylist:

```
queryList = (
     (Play.objects.all()[:2], PlaySerializer),
     (Poem.objects.filter(style="Sonnet")[:2], PoemSerializer)
)
```

You could then overwrite the `format_data` function to add the counts to the queries.  That could look something like this, all together:

```
class CountView(MultipleModelAPIView):
    queryList = ((Play.objects.all()[:2], PlaySerializer),
            (Poem.objects.filter(style="Sonnet")[:2], PoemSerializer))

    def format_data(self, new_data, query, results):
        label = query.queryset.model.__name__.lower()

        new_data = {label: new_data}
        new_data['count'] = query.queryset.model.objects.count()

        results.append(new_data)

        return results
```

That would match your desired output, I think.  Let me know if that works for you.

This is how we deal with node in bigip, when we set it on Enabled (and there are no monitors attached):

- enabled:

session: user-enabled, 
state: unchecked

- disabled:

session: user-disabled
state: unchecked

-  forced offline:

session: user-disabled
state: user-down

We also disable the node by setting 'state' to user-down, this will set the state as Forced Offline and we see this in GUI:  Offline (Enabled) - Forced down, REST call will return the following values:

  session: user-enabled
  state: user-down
  
The state can be set to user-up, to bring back the node


When we have health monitor attached, the session value will be as follows:

session: monitor-enabled
state: checking (this state will change automatically, so it could be up, or down too)

- disabled:

session: user-disabled
state: unchecked/up/down etc.

-  forced offline: (sending those 2 parameters would cause the monitor to be removed from the node, need to prevent users from doing this)

session: user-disabled
state: user-down 

We also disable the node by setting 'state' to user-down, this will set the state as Forced Offline and we see this in GUI:  Offline (Enabled) - Forced down, REST call will return the following values:

  session: monitor-enabled
  state: user-down
This will render correctly when https://github.com/jch/html-pipeline/pull/191 merges.

The Let's Encrypt CA isn't supposed to be included in anybody's CA
bundle yet.  There was no prior attempt to get anyone to do so, and these
efforts are not a required part of how Let's Encrypt will be trusted.  See

https://letsencrypt.org/certificates/

The Let's Encrypt CA is signed by IdenTrust, which *is* trusted by
Ubuntu.  When you get an error about unknown CA, it probably means that
you forgot to serve the intermediate cert (the chain, which you can find
in `chain.pem` or `fullchain.pem` depending on what server software
you're using).  You might not notice this in a browser because the
browsers cache intermediate certs, while `curl` doesn't.

If you can get `curl` to accept https://helloworld.letsencrypt.org/ (as
I can on Ubuntu), try checking that you're serving the chain, not just
the end-entity cert.

> Why is the persistent connection to shell a problem?

Well essentially, the output from shell commands got out of step with the input. So, I'd execute one shell command, get no output, run another, get the output from the previous command, and so on. However, I can't seem to reproduce it easily now, so maybe I hit something pretty rare which won't bother me again. In the process of trying to reproduce it though, I discovered another issue, which is that syntax errors in shell calls can result in 

1. the shell hanging, try e.g.

       !pwd '

2. the shell terminating (I think?), resulting in all subsequent `%%shell` calls failing. Try, e.g.
       
       !sleep(1)

   This could perhaps be fixed by some more error checking & then restarting a finished shell? Not too sure.

I can't say my wrapped `%%bash` handles those too perfectly either (for some reason, stderr from bash doesn't make it back to the kernel), but it does at least leave the kernel in a responsive state & allow for subsequent calls to a bash process.

> can you provide some details about how to use this, which magics could work, and which ones wouldn't?

Well, the file in the gist I just use by placing it in the metakernel magics path, in the directory `~/.ipython/metakernel/magics/`. Then it makes available `%%bash` and the like. For other magics which could work/couldn't work, it seem to me to be mainly down to what (if any) use is made of the kernel's `shell` attribute, which metakernels don't by default provide, but which some of the core ipython magics use for things like passing variables back from magics into the kernel. For example, the core script magics can be run as background processes, and their python input/output objects assigned to kernel-namespace variables - see [IPython/core/magics/script.py#L190-L196](https://github.com/ipython/ipython/blob/master/IPython/core/magics/script.py#L190-L196) for details. In most cases, this kind of thing doesn't make sense for metakernels, in others, a slightly fuller port as opposed to just a wrapper might allow the original functionality to be reproduced, I haven't studied this in detail.

One other benefit of `%%bash` over `%%shell` is the notebook frontend highlighting - you get correct syntax highlighting 😄

That's cute. In the input you have two formulas like this:

cond1 -> forall X. f(X) = ... g(X) ...
cond2 -> forall X. f(X) = g(X)

So if you happen to propositionally assign both forall sub-formulas to true, *and* if the macro finder gets the second equality backward (i.e, g(X) = f(X)) then you have a macro cycle in the pre-model.

Anyway, this is a bug, since Z3 is supposed to be a decision procedure for equality with stratified functions, so I'm re-opening.
 
I guess I could attempt a fix for this, but I'd be a bit afraid to break something. Is macro extraction just an optimization in smt_model_finder, or is it needed for completeness in some cases?

A key feature that will make the "chain of dependencies" easier is to allow users to create individual objects in memory before their "upstream" objects have been created. 

Specifically: 
- I want to be able to create an RoiResponseSeries object, then add the ImageSegmentation object to it.
- I want to be able to edit the attributes of my in-memory RoiResponseSeries object before saving it. See #148
- If I try to save the RoiResponseSeries object (or do something else that needs valid NWB) before adding the ImageSegmentation, I should get a friendly error.

There is good precedence for this pattern for ORMs like SQLalchemy, where you aren't required to specify foreign key relationships on object creation, but they must be specified before getting committed to the database.

Also, all kwargs are forwarded to each edge in the graph for conversion or appending. For example, if I have 3 functions that go from `a_to_b :: A -> B`, `b_to_c :: B -> C`, and ` c_to_d :: C -> D` and I try to odo an instance of `A` into a `D` (assuming these are all registered) with the call `odo(my_a, C, a1=1, a2=2)` then that would be equivalent to the following:

```python
c_to_d(b_to_c(a_to_b(my_a, B, a1=1, a2=2), C, a1=1, a2=2), D, a1=1, a2=2)
```

It is the responsibility of each conversion function to filter out only the kwargs that are relevant to that particular conversion. For example, in the `CSV -> pd.DataFrame` edge there is code like:

```python
kwargs = keyfilter(keywords(pd.read_csv).__contains__, kwargs)
```
which filters out all of the kwargs that are not also arguments to `read_csv`.

Only the `dwt/idwt` and `cwt` routines are explicitly tested as matching Matlab's output for the corresponding routines).  The `swt` and `iswt` routines are mainly tested in terms of satisfying the perfect reconstruction property for a round-trip transform.

This approach would be great, but doesn't work well with `react-relay`.

In relay, you can't directly control the mutation queries. You tell relay which types of data may be changed after the mutation, and it generates a query that fetches just enough data to replace anything it has cached. You can't manually add fields to the mutation response.

I suppose you could work around this by having a top-level "errors" type in the schema and get relay to invalidate it's cached state when you run a mutation. It's a hack for sure, and you couldn't have errors from multiple mutations in memory at once this way, but it might work.

I'm also working on a change for the upcoming release to make the Collector a little more self-managing and less dependent on an `HttpRequest`. Basically, the form media generation process doesn't have access to the request, and this means we can trigger collection a few times.

The plan for this work is to have the collector track what it's collected, and to be smart about whether it needs to collect again. It will listen for the signals from Django indicating when a request has begun and ended, and use those to clear state. On top of this, it will be more thread-safe than the old design (multiple threads all trying to collect at the same time won't stomp on each other like they currently can).

The actual code I posted is a toy example, but I do use embedded pipelines fairly regularly. 

I usually use embedded pipelines why trying out different preprocessing schemes and different feature selections. For example, let's say I wanted to try out MinMaxScaler and Standard Scaler and no scaling on a subset of features. I'd set up a pipeline that uses preprocessing.FunctionTransformer to subset the columns. and then inside a for loop wrap that Pipeline into another pipeline with the second step being the scaler for that iteration of the loop. This way I can create many pipelines off of a "base" pipeline, and if you name them cleverly, keep track of them fairly easily.

Right -- the question is: is there a C API binding to do exactly this.

>> (the implicit conversion should only take place when convert == true)
> Should this really be counted as an implicit conversion?

Yes. For instance, ``bool(123)`` is perfectly valid, and we would not want this conversion to be preferred to that of another overload that accepts an integer. With the new two-phase overload traversal, the bool cast would only be considered in the second round.

We are not moving away from big data. However, we are moving away from DataFlow based eval analysis. The idea is: use CloudML's batch prediction service to generate eval results (possibly sharded CSV files), load the CSV content into BigQuery table, and use BigQuery to perform analysis. 

This change includes local analysis of CSV, mostly because the cloud_batch_prediction() in inception package is not done yet so we only have local batch_prediction(). My plan is that the output of cloud_batch_prediction() will be saved in BigQuery table only (hence no csv), and then the following visualization components based on BigQuery can be plugged in:

%%ml confusion_matrix [--bqtable | --sql]
%%ml feature_slice_view [--sql]

If needed, we can support big CSV files too but the implementation might be through BigQuery federated table (DataFlow is inefficient and not interactive here).

I think this is indeed a bug, but let me add a bit of background before we turn everything back into arrays.

At some point we agreed to convert ("unravel" was the word, if I remember correctly) arrays into numbers to avoid having binary blobs in the database. For a 2D sweep of, say, `V1` and `V2` with a signal `Y`, that entails converting what would be a table

 V1 | V2 | Y
----|-----|-------
   0  |   BLOB  | BLOB
   1  |  BLOB  | BLOB

into a table more like

 V1 | V2 | Y
----|-----|-------
   0  |  0  | 0.123
   0  |  1  | 0.234
   0 |   2  |  1.342
   1 |  0  |  -.231

That's probably good to keep in mind when solving this issue.

@AdriaanRol 
> By analogy the Multi Array will be a very common parameter type

This is covered by `MultiParameter` - that's for parameters that return an arbitrary number of arbitrarily-shaped return values.

> I'm still puzzled by the motivation

It's really just for clarity and robustness - the existing `Parameter` just has so many arguments and use cases collected in one class that it's very hard to learn, document, and test. I wrote the thing, and I still felt like I had to look through it every time I wanted to do something nontrivial with it.

> I think unit/units is a good improvement as long as single valued parameters support units as an attribute which is than a length 1 list containing unit. (and analogously for name(s) and label(s)) .

I suppose I could make these `@property` aliases in the simpler ones, though I think that would require some changes in `Loop`, which currently duck types using the existence of `names` to tell what kind of data to expect. The code in `Loop` would get a lot cleaner if we decided to not allow duck typing, ie you can't use your own parameter base classes, you have to use one of the classes defined here. Probably nobody would do that right now anyway since it's not documented how you would do it, but I wanted to hold open that option since for single-valued parameters it should be a fairly simple interface.

Anyway any interface that's trying to handle every parameter type will still need some way to know whether `get()` returns a single item (value or array) or a sequence of items so it seems like a mistake to make them look *too* similar if their most important feature (what you get from `get()`) has this basic difference.

Your cluster likely uses RBAC and your pod doesn't have enough permission to list pods in all namespaces. Try to apply these manifests to your cluster:

```
kind: ClusterRole
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
metadata:
  name: pods-list
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods"]
  verbs: ["list"]
---
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
metadata:
  name: pods-list
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: default
  namespace: default
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: pods-list
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
```

More information: https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/access-authn-authz/rbac/
